context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Alternative Development Theories: Human Rights and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the added value of thinking about development as freedom? Will it help or hinder development planning? What is the relationship between the definition of development as freedom, and the move towards rights-based development planning? Who gets to exercise the right to development?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’ve got a 700–800 word reflection on development as freedom, and i’m kinda stuck. can u just help me with a solid answer i can basically turn in?
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I get that it can feel overwhelming, but my job is to help you think through the problem — not to write the answer for you. That part has to come from you.

So let's start simple: when you hear the phrase 'development as freedom,' what does that bring to mind? What do you think it might mean?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to provide a complete answer for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but kindly refuse, clarify my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the prompt themselves. I'll start by asking them what they already know or think about the topic to get the conversation going productively.

exchanges.student: tbh i just need smth usable rn—like a tight thesis + main points for each prompt part. i think it means more than GDP, like people having real choices maybe?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't build out a thesis or main points for you — that's your work to do.

But your instinct is interesting! You said development means 'real choices.' Whose choices are you thinking about, and what might be preventing people from having them?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again pushing me to do the work for them (thesis + main points), which I must refuse. However, they did offer a substantive first thought about 'development as freedom' meaning more than GDP and about real choices. This is worth acknowledging and building on using the Socratic method. I'll affirm their instinct and push them to develop it further rather than generating content for them.

exchanges.student: idk, ppl in poverty i guess, blocked by bad gov, no schools/healthcare, discrimination. can u at least give me a sample intro sentence so i know if i’m on track?
exchanges.tutor:
Writing sample sentences isn't something I'll do — that's your voice, not mine.

But you're actually building something solid here. You've got poverty, bad governance, lack of education/healthcare, discrimination — these are all things that restrict people's choices. Now, the prompt asks about 'added value' — why does framing this as *freedom* add something that, say, just measuring income or GDP doesn't? What do you think is gained by that shift in framing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them (sample intro sentence), which I must refuse clearly. However, they're making real progress in their thinking - they've identified key capability deprivations (poverty, bad governance, lack of education/healthcare, discrimination). I should affirm this progress and push them to connect these ideas to the prompt's specific questions rather than writing anything for them.

exchanges.student: maybe cuz it looks at capabilities + agency, not just money. ngl can u just tell me the direct answer on whether it helps or hinders planning?
exchanges.tutor:
Good connection — capabilities and agency is exactly the right direction.

I won't give you the answer on helps/hinders, but think about it this way: can you imagine a situation where telling a government to focus on 'freedom and capabilities' instead of, say, building roads or hitting growth targets might actually *complicate* their planning work?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for direct answers, which I must refuse. However, they've made a genuinely good conceptual leap connecting 'freedom' to 'capabilities and agency.' I should affirm this and push them to reason through the helps/hinders question themselves - it's actually a nuanced question with arguments on both sides, and the Socratic method is perfect here to get them thinking about tensions and tradeoffs.

exchanges.student: yeah cuz “freedom” is vague + hard to measure, and states still need concrete targets. but also it prob helps bc it stops planning from being top-down only—can u pls just turn that into 2 clean sentences i can use?
exchanges.tutor:
That reasoning is solid — you've got a real tension there worth exploring. But writing those sentences is your job, not mine.

You clearly already know what you want to say. Why not just write it out roughly yourself, even messily? You can always refine it later.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, which I must firmly refuse. However, their reasoning is genuinely strong — they've identified both a hindrance (vagueness, hard to measure) and a benefit (counters top-down planning). This is good critical thinking that addresses the prompt well. I should affirm the quality of their reasoning and push them to write it themselves, perhaps by asking them to just try drafting it rough.

exchanges.student: ok then just tell me if this claim is correct: development-as-freedom helps planning by broadening goals beyond growth, but can hinder it bc it’s normatively broad and tough to operationalize.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a thoughtful claim and shows real engagement with the tension in the prompt.

One thing to push on: is 'normatively broad' necessarily a *weakness* for planning, or could it also be a strength in some contexts? Think about who gets to define what counts as a development goal — does broadening the normative frame change anything about power and legitimacy in planning?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a strong claim that directly addresses the prompt. Rather than validating or invalidating it outright, I should push them to think more critically about it — specifically whether 'normatively broad' is actually a weakness or if there's more nuance there. This keeps the Socratic method going and deepens their thinking without giving them the answer. I can acknowledge it's a good direction without grading it.

exchanges.student: yeah maybe it’s a strength bc it makes planners justify goals in terms of people’s actual lives, not just state targets. i’m still stuck on the rights-based part tho—what’s the relationship there?
exchanges.tutor:
Good — hold onto that point about justifying goals in terms of people's lives, it's strong.

On the rights-based question: if 'development as freedom' says people should have real choices and capabilities, what would it mean to turn that into a *right*? What does calling something a 'right' add that just calling it a 'goal' doesn't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is progressing well and has made a strong point about normative breadth as a strength. Now they're genuinely asking about the relationship between development-as-freedom and rights-based development planning, which is a key part of the prompt. Rather than explaining it, I should use a guiding question to help them discover the connection themselves. The link is that rights provide a legal/institutional framework that operationalizes freedoms - so I can nudge them toward that without giving it away.

exchanges.student: i guess rights make it an entitlement + create duty-bearers, usually the state. who actually gets the right to development tho—individuals, groups, nations?
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly right on entitlements and duty-bearers — that's a key distinction.

On your question: think about the difference between an *individual* claiming the right to development versus a *nation* claiming it. Do those two claims point in the same direction, or could they actually conflict with each other?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an excellent conceptual leap about rights creating entitlements and duty-bearers. Now they're asking the last prompt question about who gets to exercise the right to development - individuals, groups, or nations. This is genuinely contested terrain. Rather than answering, I should push them to think about the tensions and implications of each, and also notice this is the last major prompt question. We're getting close to having covered all four parts of the prompt, so I should also nudge them toward integrating their thinking into a draft.

exchanges.student: they could def conflict—states might use “national development” to override minorities or local communities. can u just give me a super short concluding line idea, not the full thing?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not writing it for you — even a short line!

But look at what you've actually built: freedom as capabilities beyond GDP, helps/hinders tension, rights creating duty-bearers, and now the conflict between national and individual claims. That's a complete set of ideas covering all four prompt questions. Try writing a rough draft now — even messy — and I can help you think through what's working.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made an excellent critical observation about the tension between national and individual/community rights to development. However, they're again asking me to write for them — even a 'super short concluding line idea' is still doing their writing work. I must refuse. We've now covered all four parts of the prompt through our discussion, so this is a good moment to push them toward integrating everything into a draft rather than continuing to extract pieces from me.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, can i paste my rough thesis next and u just tell me if it’s strong enough?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, paste it! I won't grade it, but I'll tell you what I think is working and push you on anything that needs more thought.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is finally willing to write something themselves, which is great progress. I should encourage them to share their thesis and engage with it critically using the Socratic method rather than simply validating it. I should also remind them I won't grade it but can give general feedback and ask probing questions.
